messages.content: You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Please analyze TikTok's Terms of Service.

Source: https://www.tiktok.com/legal/page/us/terms-of-service/en

---
At any time, your account can be terminated without explanation and without warning by the service.

The Service is able to access, scan, review or read all user communications, including private messages.

Location-based information is collected and may be used and/or shared with third parties for purposes not directly related to the services provided.

Your personal data is or may be given to third parties nonessential to the service's operation. This could be a business partner that receives your data to provide targeted advertisements.

This service forces users to use their own contracted arbitrator and forces users to waive their rights to jury trial.

You waive your right to a class action.

Your biometric data is collected.

Your personal data may be sold or otherwise transferred as part of a bankruptcy proceeding or other type of financial transaction.

If the service gets acquired or is involved in a merger, your personal data may be transferred or sold.
---

⚠️ HIGH RISK FLAGS — TikTok received a Grade E (worst) rating from ToS;DR reviewers.

🔴 Account Deleted Without Warning — TikTok can terminate your account at any time, for any reason, with no explanation and no notice.

🔴 Private Messages Can Be Read — TikTok can access, scan, and review all your communications on the platform, including private messages.

🔴 Biometric Data Collected — TikTok collects biometric data, which can include facial recognition information from videos you upload or watch.

🔴 No Lawsuit, No Class Action — You are forced into private arbitration and waive your right to a jury trial and any class action lawsuit against TikTok.

🔴 Data Sold If TikTok Is Acquired — If TikTok is bought or goes through bankruptcy, your personal data can be sold or transferred to the new owner.

⚠️ MEDIUM RISK

🟡 Location Data Shared With Third Parties — Precise GPS location is collected and shared with third parties beyond what is needed to run the app.

🟡 Personal Data Shared With Advertisers — Your data goes to business partners for targeted advertising, not just to run TikTok itself.

✅ SUMMARY
TikTok is rated Grade E — among the worst ToS of any major platform. The combination of biometric data collection, private message scanning, forced arbitration, and no-warning account deletion makes this one of the most aggressive ToS documents in consumer tech. Use with awareness that virtually everything you do on the app is collected and can be shared.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'This service forces users to use their own contracted arbitrator and forces users to waive their rights to jury trial. You waive your right to a class action.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Dispute Resolution
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok explicitly requires all disputes to go through private arbitration — you cannot sue in court, and you cannot join a class action with other affected users. Private arbitration heavily favors large companies over individual users, and removing the class action option means TikTok faces no collective accountability even if it harms millions of users the same way.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'The Service is able to access, scan, review or read all user communications, including private messages, which may contain personal or sensitive data.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Privacy / Content Moderation
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok reserves the right to read your private messages. Content you send to friends or in private chats is not truly private — the platform can scan and review it at will. This is a significant privacy concern for anyone sharing sensitive information through the app's messaging features.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'Your biometric data is collected. This includes but is not limited to facial recognition data.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok collects biometric data including facial recognition information. This is among the most sensitive personal data that exists — it cannot be changed like a password, and once collected, can be used for identification purposes far beyond the app. This has been a central concern in national security debates about TikTok.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'Your personal data may be sold or otherwise transferred as part of a bankruptcy proceeding, merger, reorganization or sale of assets.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Third-Party Sharing
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: If TikTok is acquired, sold, or goes bankrupt, all your personal data — including your videos, messages, location history, and biometrics — transfers to the new owner. You have no say in who ends up with your data or what they do with it.

You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Please analyze Instagram's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.

Source: https://help.instagram.com/termsofuse and https://help.instagram.com/402411646841720

---
Instagram does NOT claim ANY ownership rights in the text, files, images, photos, video, sounds, or other materials that you post on or through the Instagram Services.

By displaying or publishing any Content on or through the Instagram Services, you hereby grant to Instagram a non-exclusive, fully paid and royalty-free, worldwide, limited license to use, modify, delete from, add to, publicly perform, publicly display, reproduce and translate such Content, including without limitation distributing part or all of the Site in any media formats through any media channels.

We reserve the right to modify or terminate the Instagram service for any reason, without notice at any time.

We reserve the right to alter these Terms of Use at any time. What constitutes a 'material change' will be determined at our sole discretion.

We may share User Content and your information with businesses that are legally part of the same group of companies that Instagram is part of, or that become part of that group (Affiliates). Affiliates may use this information to help provide, understand, and improve the Service and Affiliates' own services.

Following termination or deactivation of your account, Instagram, its Affiliates, or its Service Providers may retain information (including your profile information) and User Content for a commercially reasonable time for backup, archival, and/or audit purposes.
---

⚠️ HIGH RISK FLAGS — Instagram (owned by Meta)

🔴 Broad Content License — Although Instagram doesn't claim ownership, they take a worldwide, royalty-free license to use, modify, reproduce, and distribute your photos and videos across any media channel. This means your content can appear in ads or other contexts.

🔴 Service Can Be Terminated Without Notice — Instagram can shut down the platform or your account at any time, for any reason, with no warning.

🔴 Terms Can Change at Instagram's Discretion — Instagram decides unilaterally what counts as a 'material change' to the terms, giving them broad power to alter the agreement without meaningful notification.

⚠️ MEDIUM RISK

🟡 Data Shared Across All Meta Companies — Your Instagram data flows to Facebook, WhatsApp, and all other Meta affiliates, who can use it to improve their own services and build a richer profile of you.

🟡 Data Retained After Account Deletion — Even after you delete your account, Instagram and its partners may keep your data for backup and audit purposes for an undefined 'commercially reasonable' time.

✅ LOW RISK

🟢 You Keep Ownership of Your Content — Instagram explicitly does not claim copyright ownership over what you post.

✅ SUMMARY
The cross-Meta data sharing is the most impactful clause for most users — your Instagram activity contributes to the same profile Meta uses across Facebook and its ad network. The content license is broad but standard for social media. If privacy from Meta is a concern, Instagram is not the right platform.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com):

'We may share User Content and your information (including but not limited to, information from cookies, log files, device identifiers, location data, and usage data) with businesses that are legally part of the same group of companies that Instagram is part of, or that become part of that group (Affiliates). Affiliates may use this information to help provide, understand, and improve the Service and Affiliates own services.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Third-Party Sharing
Source: Instagram Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram shares your data — including location, device info, and usage patterns — freely across all Meta-owned companies (Facebook, WhatsApp, Messenger, etc.). Each affiliate can then use that data for their own purposes. This means your Instagram behavior contributes to a single massive profile Meta builds about you across all its platforms.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Terms of Use (instagram.com):

'We reserve the right to modify or terminate the Instagram service for any reason, without notice at any time.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Account Termination / Service Availability
Source: Instagram Terms of Use (instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram can shut down your account or the entire service instantly, without any warning or reason given. For users who rely on Instagram for their business or livelihood — such as creators and small business owners — this is a serious risk with no protection or recourse.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Terms of Use (instagram.com):

'Instagram does NOT claim ANY ownership rights in the text, files, images, photos, video, sounds, or other materials that you post on or through the Instagram Services.'

Risk: LOW
Category: Intellectual Property
Source: Instagram Terms of Use (instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram clearly states it does not claim copyright ownership over your content. You keep full ownership of your photos, videos, and writing. This is a user-friendly clause, though Instagram still takes a broad usage license separately — meaning they don't own it, but they can still use it.

You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Please analyze Spotify's Terms of Service.

Source: https://www.spotify.com/legal

---
SPOTIFY TERMS CONTAIN A MANDATORY ARBITRATION PROVISION THAT REQUIRES THE USE OF ARBITRATION ON AN INDIVIDUAL BASIS TO RESOLVE DISPUTES, RATHER THAN JURY TRIALS OR ANY OTHER COURT PROCEEDINGS, OR CLASS ACTIONS OF ANY KIND.

Spotify may terminate the Agreements or suspend your access to the Spotify Service at any time, including in the event of your actual or suspected unauthorized use.

Spotify does not offer refunds upon termination of their services, either by you or Spotify.

You grant perpetual license to use anything you publish on Spotify.

The service collects data and recordings of your voice when you communicate with a service verbally and may use and share them with third parties, that may use this data for marketing or advertising.

Your data is accessible to any of Spotify's trading partners.

Spotify may transfer and process your data outside of your home country, including potentially a country in which you have fewer legal rights regarding your personal information.
---

⚠️ HIGH RISK FLAGS — Spotify

🔴 Mandatory Arbitration, No Class Actions — Spotify's ToS explicitly and prominently requires all disputes to go through individual arbitration. You cannot sue in court or join a class action lawsuit. This is written in all-caps in the actual document.

🔴 Account Terminated Without Refund — Spotify can suspend your account at any time, including for 'suspected' violations, with no refund of any remaining subscription period.

🔴 Voice Data Collected and Shared — If you use voice features, Spotify records your voice and can share those recordings with third parties for marketing and advertising.

🔴 Perpetual Content License — Anything you publish on Spotify (such as podcast content) falls under a permanent license Spotify holds, surviving even account deletion.

⚠️ MEDIUM RISK

🟡 Data Shared With Trading Partners — Your data goes to Spotify's broad network of commercial partners, not just for operating the service.

🟡 Data Transferred Internationally — Your data may be processed in countries with weaker privacy laws than your own.

✅ SUMMARY
Spotify's most aggressive clause is the mandatory arbitration, written in all-caps — a signal that even Spotify expects users to object to it. No refunds on termination and voice data sharing are also notable. For casual listeners, the practical risk is low. For podcast creators using Spotify's platform, the perpetual content license deserves close attention.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'THESE TERMS CONTAIN A MANDATORY ARBITRATION PROVISION THAT REQUIRES THE USE OF ARBITRATION ON AN INDIVIDUAL BASIS TO RESOLVE DISPUTES, RATHER THAN JURY TRIALS OR ANY OTHER COURT PROCEEDINGS, OR CLASS ACTIONS OF ANY KIND. IN ARBITRATION THERE IS LESS DISCOVERY AND APPELLATE REVIEW THAN IN COURT.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Dispute Resolution
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify writes this clause in all-caps — a deliberate legal move to ensure you cannot claim you missed it. By using Spotify, you agree that all disputes must go through private arbitration and you give up your right to a jury trial or class action. Spotify even acknowledges you get fewer procedural rights in arbitration than in court. This is one of the most consumer-unfriendly dispute clauses in mainstream streaming services.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'Spotify may terminate the Agreements or suspend your access to the Spotify Service at any time, including in the event of your actual or suspected unauthorized use of the Spotify Service. Spotify does not offer refunds upon termination.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Account Termination / Subscription & Billing
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify can cut off your access based on suspicion alone — not proven violations. And when they do, you get no refund for the remaining subscription period. If you are suspended a day after your annual plan renews, you lose that entire year's payment with no recourse.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Privacy Policy (spotify.com):

'The service collects data and recordings of your voice when you communicate with a service verbally and may use and share them with third parties, that may use this data for marketing or advertising.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy
Source: Spotify Privacy Policy (spotify.com)
Explanation: When you use Spotify's voice features, your voice is recorded and can be shared with third-party advertising companies. This goes beyond what is needed to operate voice commands — it means your actual voice recordings become advertising data. Voice data is uniquely sensitive because it can be used for voice identification.

You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Please analyze Google's Terms of Service.

Source: https://tosdr.org/en/service/217

---
Location-based information is collected and may be used and/or shared with third parties for purposes not directly related to the services provided.

At any time, your account can be terminated without explanation and without warning by the service.

The service collects data and recordings of your voice when you communicate with a service verbally and may use and share them with third parties.

Ads shown to other users may include a reference to you as an implied endorsement.

Personal data or content you share with the service may be processed to make decisions by automated means without human involvement, including AI training.

The service can view your search history and the websites you visit.

After the end of the data retention period, they may still keep data for business interests or legal obligations.

If the Government governing the Terms requests personal data of a user, they may not inform/notify the user while complying with the request.
---

⚠️ HIGH RISK FLAGS — Google

🔴 Your Activity Used in Ads Shown to Others — Google can reference you in ads shown to your contacts or network as an 'implied endorsement' of products you interact with.

🔴 Data Used for AI Training — Your content and personal data can be processed for automated decision-making and AI model training without direct human review.

🔴 Government Requests May Not Be Disclosed — If a government requests your data, Google may legally be prevented from telling you. You may never know your information was handed over.

🔴 Account Terminated Without Warning — Google can close any account — Gmail, Drive, YouTube, all Google services at once — without explanation or prior notice.

⚠️ MEDIUM RISK

🟡 Location and Voice Data Shared With Third Parties — Both location and voice recordings from Google Assistant can be shared beyond what is needed to provide the service.

🟡 Search and Browsing History Collected — Google tracks your search history and the websites you visit across its services, building an extremely detailed profile.

🟡 Data Retained Beyond Stated Periods — Even after a retention period ends, Google can keep your data for business or legal reasons.

✅ SUMMARY
Google's greatest power over you is scale — a single account termination locks you out of Gmail, Drive, YouTube, Maps, and every other Google service simultaneously. The AI training use of your content and the silent government disclosure policy are the most concerning specific clauses. Given how central Google services are to daily life, understanding these risks is important.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service (tosdr.org/en/service/217):

'If the Government governing the Terms requests personal data of a user of this service, they may not inform/notify the user while complying with the request.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / Government Disclosure
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google can hand your data to government authorities and, by law, may be prohibited from telling you this happened. You would never know your emails, documents, or search history had been reviewed. This is particularly relevant given the volume of sensitive personal information stored in Google accounts.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'Ads shown to other users may include a reference to you as an implied endorsement. Might give away information about your own personal lifestyle to strangers.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / Advertising
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google can show your name or profile to other users as if you endorse a product or service — simply because you interacted with it. Your online behavior, purchases, or likes can be presented as personal recommendations to your contacts without your explicit approval for each instance.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'Personal data or content you share with the service may be processed to make decisions by automated means without human involvement. This includes profiling and Artificial Intelligence training.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / AI
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google uses your content and personal data to train AI models and make automated decisions about you through profiling. This means your emails, documents, searches, and interactions contribute to commercial AI systems — and automated systems may make decisions affecting your experience without any human reviewing them.

You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Please compare the dispute resolution clauses from TikTok and Spotify.

TikTok (tiktok.com/legal/page/us/terms-of-service/en):
'This service forces users to use their own contracted arbitrator and forces users to waive their rights to jury trial. You waive your right to a class action.'

Spotify (spotify.com/legal):
'THESE TERMS CONTAIN A MANDATORY ARBITRATION PROVISION THAT REQUIRES THE USE OF ARBITRATION ON AN INDIVIDUAL BASIS TO RESOLVE DISPUTES, RATHER THAN JURY TRIALS OR ANY OTHER COURT PROCEEDINGS, OR CLASS ACTIONS OF ANY KIND.'

⚠️ BOTH HIGH RISK — Dispute Resolution Comparison

🔴 TikTok — Forces binding arbitration, waives jury trial, waives class action. The clause is written in standard text without special emphasis.

🔴 Spotify — Identical restrictions but written in ALL-CAPS in the actual document. This is a legal tactic to prove you were explicitly warned and cannot claim ignorance. Spotify even adds: 'In arbitration there is less discovery and appellate review than in court.'

📊 COMPARISON
Both services use the same core tactic — stripping your right to sue in court and join class actions. Spotify is arguably more aggressive in its presentation (all-caps, explicit acknowledgment of your reduced rights). Both effectively mean: if either company harms you or millions of users in the same way, no one can band together to hold them accountable in a public courtroom.

✅ BOTTOM LINE
This is now standard practice for large tech platforms. It is worth knowing that by signing up for either service, you are giving up significant legal rights that would otherwise protect you as a consumer.

You are a Terms of Service analyzer. When given the full text of a Terms of Service or Privacy Policy, identify risky clauses, flag them with a risk level (HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW), and provide a plain-English summary a non-lawyer can understand.

Here are account termination clauses from three real platforms. Please analyze them.

Instagram (instagram.com):
'We reserve the right to modify or terminate the Instagram service for any reason, without notice at any time.'

Spotify (spotify.com/legal):
'Spotify may terminate the Agreements or suspend your access to the Spotify Service at any time, including in the event of your actual or suspected unauthorized use.'

Google (tosdr.org/en/service/217):
'At any time, your account can be terminated without explanation and without warning by the service.'

⚠️ ALL THREE ARE HIGH RISK — Account Termination Comparison

🔴 Instagram — Can terminate the entire service or your account 'for any reason, without notice at any time.' No criteria stated, no appeal mentioned.

🔴 Spotify — Termination can happen on suspicion alone ('actual or suspected' misuse). You don't have to actually do anything wrong. No refund is offered regardless of when termination occurs.

🔴 Google — Account deleted 'without explanation and without warning.' This is particularly severe because one Google account controls Gmail, Drive, YouTube, Google Photos, Maps, and every other Google service simultaneously.

📊 WHAT THIS MEANS IN PRACTICE
All three platforms give themselves unchecked power to end your access instantly. Google's is the most impactful in daily life because of how many services it controls. Spotify's is most financially punitive because of the no-refund policy. Instagram's affects anyone using it as a business or income source.

✅ BOTTOM LINE
Never store irreplaceable data solely on any of these platforms. Always maintain backups and alternative contact methods for your audience or business contacts.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com/402411646841720):

'Following termination or deactivation of your account, Instagram, its Affiliates, or its Service Providers may retain information (including your profile information) and User Content for a commercially reasonable time for backup, archival, and/or audit purposes.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Data Privacy / Account Termination
Source: Instagram Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com)
Explanation: After you delete your Instagram account, your data — including your profile, photos, and posts — can be kept by Instagram, Meta, and their partners for an undefined 'commercially reasonable' period. There is no specific timeframe promised. Your data persists well after you believe you have left the platform.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'Where applicable and permitted under applicable law, you also agree to waive and not enforce any moral rights or equivalent rights, such as your right to be identified as the author of any User Content, including Feedback, and your right to object to derogatory treatment of such User Content.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Intellectual Property
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify asks you to give up your 'moral rights' — the legal right to be credited as the creator of content you post, and the right to object if your content is modified or used in a way that harms your reputation. For creators posting original music, podcasts, or other work, this means Spotify can alter or attribute your content without your ability to object.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'The service can view your search history and the websites you visit, which is an extremely intrusive way of collecting information that may include personal and/or sensitive data.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / Tracking
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google collects and retains your complete search history and browsing activity across its services. Over time, this creates a deeply personal record of your health concerns, financial situation, political beliefs, relationships, and daily habits. This data is used for targeted advertising and can be requested by governments.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'This service receives your precise location through GPS coordinates. Location-based information is collected and may be used and/or shared with third parties for purposes not directly related to the services provided.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / Location Tracking
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok collects your precise GPS location and can share it with third parties for purposes beyond running the app — including advertising. A short video app does not need GPS-level precision to function. This level of location tracking builds a detailed map of your physical movements that can be shared commercially.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'Your profile is combined across various products. When using multiple services from one same company, they can combine these information sources about you to build up a single richer user profile.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy / Cross-Platform Tracking
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok and its parent company ByteDance combine data about you across all their apps and services to build a comprehensive profile. Data from different apps that would seem unrelated gets merged — creating a more detailed picture of your behavior, interests, and identity than any single app could reveal alone.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'You can stop using the service and/or cancel or delete your account at any time for any reason. Moreover, the termination is reasonably fast and simple.'

Risk: LOW
Category: Account Termination
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: This is a positive clause — Google makes it easy for users to leave. You can delete your account at any time without penalties or complicated processes. This is balanced against the fact that Google can also terminate your account without warning, but at least the user-initiated exit is straightforward.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'Spotify provides a link in order to cancel your subscription to their services, which you can do at any time. However, the cancellation takes effect after the last day of the current subscription period, so if you cancel before the end of the subscription period, Spotify will not offer you a refund.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Subscription & Billing
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: You can cancel Spotify anytime, which is user-friendly. However, there are no prorated refunds — if you cancel a week into a monthly billing cycle, you pay for the full month. For annual subscribers who change their mind early in the year, this means losing a significant amount of money with no recourse.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Terms of Use (instagram.com):

'By displaying or publishing any Content on or through the Instagram Services, you hereby grant to Instagram a non-exclusive, fully paid and royalty-free, worldwide, limited license to use, modify, delete from, add to, publicly perform, publicly display, reproduce and translate such Content, including without limitation distributing part or all of the Site in any media formats through any media channels.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Intellectual Property
Source: Instagram Terms of Use (instagram.com)
Explanation: While Instagram doesn't claim ownership of your photos and videos, this clause gives them a permanent, royalty-free, worldwide license to use your content however they want — including modifying it, using it in ads, and distributing it across any media. You get no payment and have limited control over how your images appear once posted.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Privacy Policy (spotify.com):

'Your data is accessible to any of Spotify trading partners. Spotify may transfer and process your data outside of your home country, including potentially a country in which you have fewer legal rights regarding your personal information.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Third-Party Sharing / Data Privacy
Source: Spotify Privacy Policy (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify shares your personal data with a broad network of commercial trading partners and may move it to countries with weaker privacy protections than your own. In those jurisdictions, your legal rights over your data — including the right to access, correct, or delete it — may simply not apply.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service (tosdr.org/en/service/217):

'Most browsers allow users to set preferences related to cookies storage. You may choose to block all cookies (including first-party cookies), but it may impact your use of this service and you may not be able to access some or any of the features provided.'

Risk: LOW
Category: Tracking & Cookies
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google discloses that you can block cookies through your browser, though doing so may break some features. This is a fair and transparent disclosure — the company acknowledges user control exists even if exercising it has tradeoffs. Cookie control options are a meaningful, if limited, privacy tool.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'Content you post may be edited by the service for any reason. This service can edit any content on the service for whatever the reason and at their sole discretion.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Content / Intellectual Property
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok can modify your videos, captions, or other content for any reason without asking you. Combined with their broad content license, this means your original work can be altered and redistributed in a modified form you never approved or intended.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'The service may anonymize the personal data after its retention period is over and use it for analytical or research purposes. After the end of the data retention period, they may still keep data for business interests or legal obligations.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Data Privacy
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Even after Google's stated data retention period ends, your data may be kept longer for business reasons — and then 'anonymized' rather than deleted, persisting indefinitely in research and analytics. True anonymization is technically difficult and not always permanent, meaning your data may continue to be used long after you expect it to be gone.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'This service retains rights to your content even after you stop using your account.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Intellectual Property
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify keeps their usage license to your content — podcasts, playlists, or other submissions — even after you close your account. Deleting your account does not revoke the rights you granted Spotify. Your content can continue to be used commercially after you leave the platform.

Clause from Instagram's Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com):

'We also may share your information as well as information from tools like cookies, log files, and device identifiers and location data, with third-party organizations that help us provide the Service to you (Service Providers). Our Service Providers will be given access to your information as is reasonably necessary to provide the Service under reasonable confidentiality terms.'

Risk: LOW
Category: Third-Party Sharing
Source: Instagram Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram shares your data with service providers who help run the platform — such as hosting companies, analytics firms, and payment processors. This is standard and necessary for most platforms to operate. The key positive here is that access is limited to what is 'reasonably necessary' and providers are bound by confidentiality agreements.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'The service may use your personal data for marketing, such as sending you personalised offerings. The service logs the URL of the last web page you visited before visiting the Site and may use that information to gather more data about you.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Tracking & Advertising
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google tracks the referring URL — the page you were on before visiting a Google service — to collect additional context about you. Combined with Google's existing data, this referral tracking enriches your advertising profile. It means Google is not just tracking what you do on Google, but also gathering signals about where you came from across the web.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'Specific content can be deleted without reason and may be removed without prior notice. This service can delete specific content at their sole discretion, without giving a specific reason.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Content Moderation
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok can remove any video or content without telling you why. While platforms need moderation powers, 'sole discretion' with no required explanation means removals can be arbitrary. For creators whose income depends on content staying live, unexplained takedowns with no stated appeal process are a meaningful business risk.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'This service is only available for use individually and non-commercially. Spotify's products may be subject to export control and economic sanctions laws.'

Risk: LOW
Category: User Conduct
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Spotify is intended for personal, non-commercial use — meaning you cannot play it in your store or restaurant without a business license. This is a standard clause for consumer music services. The export control mention affects users in sanctioned countries, not typical US or EU users.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Instagram's Terms of Use (instagram.com):

'We reserve the right to alter these Terms of Use at any time. What constitutes a material change will be determined at our sole discretion, in good faith and using common sense and reasonable judgement.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Terms Modification
Source: Instagram Terms of Use (instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram decides for itself what counts as a significant enough change to notify you about. If they redefine a major data sharing practice but decide internally it is not 'material,' they can make the change without telling you. The circular logic — they judge whether their own changes require notice — removes any real protection for users.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'You can stop using the service and/or cancel or delete your account at any time for any reason. The service has provided a way for you to export or download your data.'

Risk: LOW
Category: User Rights
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google provides both a clear exit path and data export tools (Google Takeout). This is one of the more user-friendly aspects of Google's ToS — you can leave whenever you want and take your data with you. This is a meaningful positive compared to many platforms that make leaving difficult or data recovery impossible.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Spotify's Terms of Service (spotify.com/legal):

'The service may change its terms at any time, but the user will receive notification of the changes.'

Category: Terms Modification
Source: Spotify Terms of Service (spotify.com)
Explanation: Unlike some platforms that change terms silently, Spotify commits to notifying users when terms change. This is a relatively user-friendly approach to terms modification — you will at least be informed before changes take effect, giving you the opportunity to review and decide whether to continue using the service.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'You are tracked via web beacons, tracking pixels and device fingerprinting. Tracking technologies are employed on users and the service assigns a unique ID to a browser or device to track its behaviour.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Tracking & Cookies
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok uses device fingerprinting — a technique that creates a unique identifier from your device's characteristics — to track you even if you block cookies or clear your browser. Unlike cookies, fingerprinting cannot easily be blocked or reset. This enables persistent tracking across sessions and devices.

Clause from Instagram's Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com):

'This may include responding to legal requests from jurisdictions outside of the United States where we have a good faith belief that the response is required by law in that jurisdiction, affects users in that jurisdiction, and is consistent with internationally recognized standards.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Government Disclosure
Source: Instagram Privacy Policy (help.instagram.com)
Explanation: Instagram can respond to legal requests from foreign governments — not just US authorities — if they believe it is legally required. For users in countries with authoritarian governments, this means Instagram could hand local authorities information about activists, journalists, or dissidents who use the platform.

Clause from Spotify's Privacy Policy (spotify.com):

'The service can sell or otherwise transfer your personal data as part of a bankruptcy proceeding or other type of financial transaction.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Third-Party Sharing
Source: Spotify Privacy Policy (spotify.com)
Explanation: If Spotify is sold, merged, or goes bankrupt, your personal data — listening history, voice data, payment information, and behavioral profiles — becomes a transferable asset. The new owner has no obligation to honor Spotify's current privacy commitments, and you have no opt-out rights when this happens.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Google's Terms of Service:

'Some services store data on you even if you did not provide that data yourself. For example, users might provide access to their address book, after which a service might also store the phone numbers of that person contacts.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Data Privacy
Source: Google Terms of Service
Explanation: Google can collect data about you from other users — if someone uploads their contacts list and your number is in it, Google now has your information even though you never agreed to anything. This 'shadow profile' building means Google may have data on people who have never created a Google account.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from TikTok's Terms of Service (tiktok.com):

'The service may update the Terms without notice, but if they significantly change the agreements in a manner that importantly and/or negatively affects the way the service is used, users will be notified.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Terms Modification
Source: TikTok Terms of Service (tiktok.com)
Explanation: TikTok will notify you of 'significant' changes but decides itself what rises to that level. Minor-seeming changes to data practices or privacy settings might not trigger notification even if they meaningfully affect your experience. This is a middle-ground approach — better than no notification at all, but not as strong as requiring notice for all changes.

Please analyze Uber's Terms of Service.

Source: https://www.uber.com/legal/en/document/?name=general-terms-of-use and https://tosdr.org/en/service/252

---
PLEASE READ THESE TERMS OF SERVICE CAREFULLY AS THEY CONTAIN PROVISIONS THAT GOVERN HOW YOU CAN BRING CLAIMS AND/OR LAWSUITS BETWEEN YOU AND UBER, INCLUDING THE ARBITRATION AGREEMENT IN SECTION 2 BELOW. THE ARBITRATION AGREEMENT REQUIRES YOU TO RESOLVE ALL DISPUTES WITH UBER ON AN INDIVIDUAL BASIS AND, WITH LIMITED EXCEPTIONS, THROUGH FINAL AND BINDING ARBITRATION.

At any time, your account can be terminated without explanation and without warning by the service.

The service collects and shares your data with third parties for advertising or marketing.

Your personal data is or may be given to third parties nonessential to the service's operation.

The service collects different kinds of personal data, such as name, demographic information, postal address, biometric data, device information, IDs, contacts, call logs.

The service may use tracking technologies in third-party websites to track your online activity while you aren't visiting their site.

The existence and content of the arbitration proceedings shall remain strictly confidential and shall not be disclosed to any third party.
---

⚠️ HIGH RISK FLAGS — Uber

🔴 Mandatory Arbitration, Written in All-Caps — Like Spotify, Uber highlights its mandatory arbitration clause in all-caps to ensure you cannot claim ignorance. All disputes must be resolved individually through private arbitration — no class actions, no jury trial.

🔴 Arbitration Is Confidential — Any dispute you have with Uber goes through a secret process. Neither side can discuss it publicly. This prevents other users from learning about Uber's conduct and shields the company from reputational accountability.

🔴 Account Terminated Without Warning — Uber can deactivate your account at any time without explanation. For drivers, this means sudden loss of income with no recourse.

🔴 Biometric and Sensitive Data Collected — Uber collects biometric data, contacts, call logs, and device identifiers — far more than needed to book a ride.

⚠️ MEDIUM RISK

🟡 Cross-Site Tracking — Uber tracks your online activity on third-party websites, not just within the Uber app.

🟡 Data Shared With Advertisers — Your personal data goes to marketing partners beyond what is needed to operate the service.

✅ SUMMARY
Uber's most notable clause is the confidential mandatory arbitration — not only can you not sue in court, but if you do arbitrate, the outcome stays secret. For drivers especially, the no-warning deactivation policy combined with no class action rights creates significant vulnerability. Note: Uber has since removed mandatory arbitration specifically for sexual assault and harassment claims, but it remains in force for all other disputes.
You are a Terms of Service analyzer. Classify each clause as LOW, MEDIUM, or HIGH risk, identify the category, and provide a plain-English explanation.

Clause from Uber's Terms of Service (uber.com/legal):

'THE ARBITRATION AGREEMENT REQUIRES YOU TO RESOLVE ALL DISPUTES WITH UBER ON AN INDIVIDUAL BASIS AND, WITH LIMITED EXCEPTIONS, THROUGH FINAL AND BINDING ARBITRATION. THE EXISTENCE AND CONTENT OF THE ARBITRATION PROCEEDINGS SHALL REMAIN STRICTLY CONFIDENTIAL.'

Risk: HIGH
Category: Dispute Resolution
Source: Uber Terms of Service (uber.com)
Explanation: Uber's arbitration clause is written in all-caps and adds a confidentiality requirement on top. Not only can you not sue in court or join a class action — if you do arbitrate, the entire process and outcome must be kept secret. This means Uber can settle thousands of similar complaints without those outcomes ever becoming public knowledge, removing a key deterrent against repeat misconduct.

Risk: HIGH
Category: Account Termination / Data Privacy
Source: Uber Terms of Service (uber.com)
Explanation: Uber collects an unusually broad range of personal data — including biometrics, contacts, and call logs — while simultaneously reserving the right to delete your account with no warning and no explanation. For drivers who depend on Uber for income, sudden deactivation combined with this level of data collection creates serious power imbalance with no meaningful appeal process.

Explanation: Uber tracks your behavior across the broader web — not just inside the Uber app. Even when you are browsing unrelated websites, Uber's tracking technologies can follow you. A ride-sharing app tracking your full internet activity goes well beyond what is needed to book or drive rides.

Clause from Uber's Privacy Notice (uber.com/global/en/privacy-notice-drivers-delivery-people):

'Uber may share your data if we believe it is required by applicable law, regulation, operating license or agreement, legal process or governmental request, or where the disclosure is otherwise appropriate due to safety or similar concerns. This includes sharing data with law enforcement officials, public health officials, other government authorities, or insurance companies.'

Risk: MEDIUM
Category: Government Disclosure
Source: Uber Privacy Notice (uber.com)
Explanation: Uber has a broad standard for when it shares your data with authorities — not just when legally compelled, but whenever it believes sharing is 'appropriate due to safety or similar concerns.' The vague 'similar concerns' language gives Uber significant discretion to share data with law enforcement beyond clear legal requirements. For gig workers in particular, this is worth understanding.
